rules.description: The CulDeSac pattern must be used when a service needs to publish domain events without expecting a return value from subscribers.
Event services are always foundation services -- they sit at the boundary between the domain and the event infrastructure.
Event services must not depend on other services -- only on the event broker.
Events must be used to decouple services across bounded contexts without creating direct service-to-service dependencies.
The event broker interface must be named IEventBroker.
The event broker implementation must be named EventBroker (partial class).
Entity-specific broker implementation members must be defined in a dedicated partial file: EventBroker_[Entity].cs.
Entity-specific broker interface members must be defined in a dedicated partial file: IEventBroker_[Entity].cs.
The broker must expose exactly two operations per entity: Publish[Entity]Async and SubscribeTo[Entity]Event.
Broker operations must be thin pass-throughs to the underlying event infrastructure -- no business logic lives in the broker.
Event services must implement exactly two operations per entity: Publish[Entity]Async and SubscribeTo[Entity]Event.
Publish[Entity]Async must be async (ValueTask) and validate the entity before calling the broker.
SubscribeTo[Entity]Event must be synchronous (void) and validate the handler before calling the broker.
Event services must NOT inject a logging broker -- event services have no logging dependency.
Event services must be internal partial classes.
Event services must use TryCatch delegates to separate exception handling from business logic.
Publish operations must validate the entity is not null before calling the broker.
Subscribe operations must validate the event handler is not null before calling the broker.
Null entity violations must throw NullEventException -- not InvalidEntityException.
Null handler violations must throw NullEventHandlerException.
Null checks on entity and handler are circuit-breaking: throw immediately, do not continue or collect further errors.
Validation exceptions from event services must be wrapped in [Entity]EventValidationException.
Event services must use two TryCatch delegates: ReturningNothingFunction (sync) and ReturningValueTaskFunction (async).
All unexpected exceptions must be wrapped in FailedEventServiceException before being rethrown.
FailedEventServiceException must be wrapped in [Entity]EventServiceException before leaving the service boundary.
Event services do NOT catch DependencyValidation, Dependency, or CriticalDependency exceptions -- they do not call HTTP or storage APIs.
FailedEventServiceException must carry the original exception as innerException and the original exception's Data collection.
The event service must be split into three partial files: main (.cs), .Validations.cs, and .Exceptions.cs.
The event broker must be split into four files: IEventBroker.cs, IEventBroker_[Entity].cs, EventBroker.cs, EventBroker_[Entity].cs.
Test files must follow the partial split: root, Logic.Publish, Logic.Subscribe, Validations.Publish, Validations.Subscribe, Exceptions.Publish, Exceptions.Subscribe.
The root test file must contain only: mock declarations, dependency instantiation, and helper/filler methods.
The event service interface must be named I[Entity]EventService.
The event service implementation must be named [Entity]EventService.
Publish operations must be named Publish[Entity]Async.
Subscribe operations must be named SubscribeTo[Entity]Event.
The model exception namespace must be [Namespace].Models.Foundations.[Entities].Exceptions.

Exception types must follow the naming pattern: Null[Entity]EventException, Null[Entity]EventHandlerException, Failed[Entity]EventServiceException, [Entity]EventValidationException, [Entity]EventServiceException.
Test SubscribeTo[Entity]Events (happy path) first.
Test Publish[Entity]Async (happy path) second.
Test validation failures third (null handler, null entity).
Test service exceptions fourth (unexpected errors in Publish and Subscribe).
Event service tests must NOT declare a loggingBrokerMock -- event services have no logging broker.
Subscribe handler mocks must be declared as Mock<Func<[Entity], ValueTask>>.
Always end every event service test with eventBrokerMock.VerifyNoOtherCalls().
Use Times.Once for expected broker calls and Times.Never for broker calls that must not occur due to validation failures.

The DI lifetime of IEventBroker and EventBroker must match the requirements of the event infrastructure in use -- singleton is required for in-memory infrastructure (such as LeVent) where subscription state lives in client instances; for external infrastructure (such as Azure Service Bus or EventHighway), follow the client library's lifetime recommendations, which are typically singleton for connection reuse and message processor lifecycle.
I[Entity]EventService and [Entity]EventService must be registered with a lifetime that matches their IEventBroker dependency -- never register the service with a longer lifetime than the broker.
Every subscribing orchestration service must expose SubscribeTo[Entity]Events (plural) as the public activation method that wraps the event service's SubscribeTo[Entity]Event (singular).

SubscribeTo[Entity]Events must be called at application startup via app.ApplicationServices.GetService<I[Entity]OrchestrationService>() (Startup.cs) or app.Services.GetService<>() (minimal API) -- subscriptions are not self-activating.
Startup activation calls must appear in Configure() or the minimal API equivalent -- never inside a controller action, service method, or middleware.
